content: I want to make a really good spaghetti carbonara tonight. What's the secret to getting the sauce silky instead of scrambled?

The trick is temperature control. Whisk the eggs and grated Pecorino together off the heat, then toss them with the hot pasta away from the burner, adding a splash of starchy pasta water to loosen it. The residual heat cooks the eggs into a creamy sauce without scrambling them.
Should I use guanciale or pancetta, and do I need cream?

You're making a classic Italian dish, I presume? Both guanciale and pancetta are excellent choices, but they have slightly different flavor profiles. Guanciale has a more delicate, unsmoked flavor, while pancetta is often smoked and has a bolder taste. If you want a more authentic carbonara experience, guanciale might be the way to go. But if you can't find guanciale or prefer a stronger flavor, pancetta is a great substitute.

As for the cream, traditional carbonara doesn't actually contain any cream. The creaminess comes from the eggs, cheese, and pasta water. Adding cream can make the dish richer, but it can also make it feel heavier and less authentic. If you want to stay true to the original recipe, you can omit the cream and focus on getting the egg and cheese mixture just right. That being said, if you're looking for a creamier sauce, a small amount of cream or even half-and-half can be added to enhance the texture. What's your personal preference?

content:
The key to a perfect egg and cheese mixture is to temper the eggs just right, so they cook slowly and evenly, creating a creamy sauce. I like to whisk the eggs with a fork, then slowly add some of the hot pasta water to the eggs, whisking constantly, to warm them up. This way, when you combine the eggs with the hot pasta, they'll cook just enough to create a silky texture, without scrambling.

As for the cheese, Parmigiano-Reggiano is the classic choice, and for good reason - its nutty, salty flavor is a perfect complement to the guanciale and eggs. That being said, you can definitely experiment with mixing it with other Italian cheeses. I've had great results with a combination of Parmigiano-Reggiano and Pecorino Romano - the Pecorino adds a nice tanginess and a bit of sharpness to balance out the richness of the dish. Just be sure to use a high-quality, aged cheese, as the flavor will be more pronounced and nuanced.

One thing to keep in mind is that the cheese should be grated just before using, so it's nice and fine. This will help it melt evenly and distribute its flavor throughout the dish. And don't overdo it with the cheese - you want just enough to add depth and richness, without overpowering the other ingredients.

Now, I'm curious - have you thought about adding any other ingredients to your carbonara, or are you keeping it traditional with just guanciale, eggs, cheese, and pasta? Some people like to add some black pepper or parsley, but I think those can be a bit divisive - what's your take on it?

One thing I've found is that using a ratio of about 2:1 aquafaba to tofu works really well, as it allows the aquafaba to add a light, airy texture while the tofu provides a richer, more custard-like element. I've also found that adding a bit of tapioca starch or arrowroot powder can help to thicken the mixture and give it a more egg-like consistency.

As for the tempeh and seitan, I've been using a combination of the two to create a really meaty, savory flavor. For the seitan, I've been using a marinating process that involves soaking it in a mixture of soy sauce, maple syrup, smoked paprika, and garlic for at least a few hours. This gives the seitan a really rich, deep flavor that's similar to bacon. To cook the seitan, I've been using a combination of pan-frying and baking, which helps to give it a crispy exterior and a tender interior.

One tip I've found is to use a cast-iron skillet to cook the seitan, as it retains heat really well and can help to get a nice crust on the outside. I've also been experimenting with adding different flavorings to the seitan, such as dried mushrooms or seaweed, to give it a more umami flavor.

I'm really interested in your use of cashew-based cream sauce - I've been using a similar sauce made with soaked cashews and lemon juice, but I've been wanting to try adding some other ingredients to give it a bit more depth.
